pipeline_verified.nextest_benten_graph: 55 pass / 14 expected reds (G3/G6/SC1 scope) + 1 flagged test-authoring bug

findings.claim: Latent footgun: NodeStore blanket impl on RedbBackend silently bypasses index maintenance when dispatched via the trait (generic bound, explicit `<Self as NodeStore>::put_node`, or future dyn dispatch).
findings.detail:
The inherent `RedbBackend::put_node` maintains `LABEL_INDEX_TABLE` + `PROP_INDEX_TABLE` atomically in the same write transaction as the node body. But `impl<T: KVBackend> NodeStore for T` also provides a trait-level `put_node` that only writes `n:CID` via `KVBackend::put` — no index touch. Rust's method resolution prefers the inherent method ONLY when resolving `b.put_node(&n)` on a concrete `RedbBackend`. Any of these paths bypass it:
  1. Generic bound: `fn f<T: NodeStore>(store: &T, n: &Node) { store.put_node(n); }`
  2. Explicit trait: `<RedbBackend as NodeStore>::put_node(&b, &n)`
  3. Future `dyn NodeStore` if trait becomes object-safe (currently not, owing to `type Error`).
The audit-flagged implementer note (G2-B #3) calls this 'intentional asymmetry — indexes are redb-specific via MultimapTable' but it is not documented as a hazard on the trait, on the blanket impl, or on the inherent method. Silent index-miss is the class of bug that persists through unit tests (tests use `b.put_node` on the concrete type → inherent wins) and only manifests when a real consumer reaches through a trait-object or generic boundary in G7 or later.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) remove the blanket `NodeStore for T: KVBackend` impl and move NodeStore into an inherent-only shape on RedbBackend (acceptable for Phase 1 since RedbBackend is the only backend), or (b) add a warning to the NodeStore trait doc + the blanket impl's doc block explicitly calling out that non-redb backends do NOT maintain label/property indexes and that downstream consumers must either use the concrete RedbBackend or re-implement index maintenance. Also add a regression test along the lines of `fn uses_via_trait<T: NodeStore<Error=GraphError>>(...)` that documents what the caller sees. The cleanest fix long-term is a separate `IndexedNodeStore` supertrait that only RedbBackend implements, so the type system enforces the guarantee.

findings.claim: Public docstrings reference private items, producing four cargo-doc warnings — user-facing docs render with broken links.
findings.detail:
`cargo doc -p benten-graph --no-deps` produces:
  1. `warning: unresolved link to \`RedbBackend\`` in indexes.rs:1 (module docstring links to a cross-crate-private type)
  2. `warning: public documentation for \`put_node\` links to private item \`LABEL_INDEX_TABLE\``
  3. `warning: public documentation for \`put_node\` links to private item \`PROP_INDEX_TABLE\``
  4. `warning: unresolved link to \`1\`` in bin/write_canonical_and_exit.rs:3 (the `argv[1]` pattern without escape)
The `indexes.rs` module is `pub mod indexes` in lib.rs but `LABEL_INDEX_TABLE` / `PROP_INDEX_TABLE` are `pub(crate)`; the public `put_node` docstring can't link to them. The module-level indexes.rs docstring is only visible via `pub mod`, and it tries to link `RedbBackend` which lives in a different module. These warnings block any future `-D warnings` on docs.
findings.suggested_fix:
1. In redb_backend.rs:297-299, replace `[\`LABEL_INDEX_TABLE\`]` + `[\`PROP_INDEX_TABLE\`]` with plain-text names or describe in prose: 'inserts one multimap entry per (node, label) pair into the crate-private label index, and one per (node, label, prop_name) triple into the crate-private property-value index'.
2. In indexes.rs:1, change `[\`RedbBackend\`]` to `[\`crate::RedbBackend\`]` or plain text.
3. In bin/write_canonical_and_exit.rs:3, escape: `argv\[1\]`.
Consider adding `#![deny(rustdoc::broken_intra_doc_links)]` to lib.rs after cleanup to prevent regressions.

findings.claim: DurabilityMode::Group silently collapses to Immediate; the collapse is visible only in a non-docstring comment in `to_redb_durability`. Users reading the public enum variant believe they are getting grouped fsync and silently pay per-commit fsync instead.
findings.detail: Audit concern #1 confirmed. `DurabilityMode::Group` docstring (backend.rs:60-61) reads 'Higher throughput, bounded tail latency on the fsync flush' with no caveat. The collapse to `Durability::Immediate` happens inside `to_redb_durability` (redb_backend.rs:67-72) in a private function's internal doc. A user who sets `Group` for a benchmark workload will see no throughput improvement, silently, and have no runtime indication why. This is especially sharp because DurabilityMode is the type users interact with when tuning — misleading performance documentation is as harmful as misleading correctness documentation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `# Phase 1 note` paragraph to `DurabilityMode::Group`'s docstring (backend.rs:61) explicitly stating: 'Phase 1 / redb v4 note: `Group` currently collapses to `Immediate` semantics — redb v4 only exposes `Durability::Immediate` and `Durability::None`. The enum variant is retained so Phase 2 can wire true group-commit without an API break.' Optionally emit a single-shot `tracing::warn!` on backend construction when the requested mode is `Group` and has been collapsed, gated behind the optional `tracing` feature.

findings.claim: `path.exists()` pre-check on `open_existing` is a TOCTOU window; it is also the only reason the error message is useful. Acceptable for Phase 1 but callout should note the race isn't a defect-to-fix at current scope.
findings.detail: Audit concern #2. `path.exists()` races with filesystem mutations from other processes. In Phase 1 (single-user local store, exclusive lock enforced by redb anyway on open) this is irrelevant. The fallback path — letting `Database::open` error and translating opaque redb errors — would produce messages like 'unable to allocate page' rather than 'backend not found'. The current design trades a race window for error-message clarity, which is the right trade for Phase 1. The docstring (lib.rs:74-79) still claims 'BackendNotFound' with a PathBuf placeholder but actually now has the full path. Minor doc drift.
findings.suggested_fix:
1. Update the `GraphError::BackendNotFound { path: std::path::PathBuf }` docstring in lib.rs:73-79 to remove the 'Phase 1 stub — the PathBuf plumbing lands in G2-B; today this is a placeholder' text — G2-B wired it.
2. Add a `# Concurrency note` paragraph to `open_existing`'s docstring calling out the path-existence check is a best-effort clarity helper, not a hard guarantee against concurrent filesystem mutations. Phase 3 P2P adds harder requirements; re-evaluate then.

findings.claim: Test-authoring bug in `open_or_create_idempotent_on_existing_file`: missing `{}` scope block causes the second `RedbBackend::open_or_create` to fail on redb's exclusive file lock. Implementer flagged but did not fix.
findings.detail:
Audit concern flagged by implementer in G2-B commit message, confirmed. The sibling test `open_existing_existing_file_succeeds` (lines 54-67) uses `{ let _first = ...; }` to drop the first handle before reopening. `open_or_create_idempotent_on_existing_file` omits the block: `let _first = RedbBackend::open_or_create(&db_path).unwrap();` lives in the outer function scope, so the second `open_or_create` call at line 77 races against the still-held redb Database handle. redb v4 uses an exclusive file lock; the second call fails with DatabaseAlreadyOpen.

This is unambiguously a test-authoring mistake (the test name and intent clearly state 'idempotent on existing file'). Fix is 2 lines — wrap the first handle in a `{}` block, same as the sibling test. Implementer chose to flag rather than edit in G2-B scope, which is the right call given the strict TDD red-phase / 'the tests ARE the specification' contract in CLAUDE.md. R5-G2 fix-pass or R4b is the right place to apply the patch with appropriate documentation in the triage log.

findings.claim: The ScanResult 'future-swap-to-lazy-iterator' affordance is partly illusory: consumers reliant on Deref/indexing/len will break when the internal representation swaps to a boxed iterator.
findings.detail:
Audit concern from G2-A #1 confirmed. The backend.rs module docstring (line 12-18) and the ScanResult doc (line 79-88) both claim the newtype 'gives Phase 2 a migration path: when a true-lazy streaming scan lands, ScanResult can wrap a boxed iterator internally without changing the public return type'. But ScanResult exposes:
- `Deref<Target=[(Vec<u8>, Vec<u8>)]>` — materialized slice reference
- `.len()` / `.is_empty()` / indexing via Deref — requires ExactSizeIterator at minimum
- `from_vec` / `into_inner` / `From<Vec<...>>` / `FromIterator` — expose Vec internals
A lazy iterator wrapper CAN maintain `IntoIterator` and `FromIterator` but CANNOT maintain `Deref<Target=[...]>` without materializing first (defeating laziness) or `len`/`is_empty` without pre-computing (ditto). The 'migration path' in practice is either (a) materialize internally anyway, erasing the iteration benefit, or (b) break every call site using Deref/indexing/len — which the R3 tests lean on heavily.

The R1 triage `P1.graph.scan-iterator` ask was for a lazy iterator return type; the implementer correctly noted that tests force a Vec-backed shape, so the full fix is deferred. The concern is that the current docstring overpromises. Phase 2 will likely have to introduce a separate `LazyScanResult` type or change the trait shape — which is fine, just not a 'migration without breaking call sites' step as claimed.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the ScanResult docstring (backend.rs:79-88) to read: 'Phase 1 uses a Vec-backed representation because every R3 consumer relies on slice-like ergonomics (len, indexing, Deref). A future lazy-streaming scan will likely require a separate return type or a trait-level split, not an in-place swap of the internal rep — existing Deref and len consumers will be source-incompatible with any streaming shape. The newtype still gives us a single type to evolve and keeps the trait surface stable for Phase 1.' Same honesty update on the module-level docstring (lines 12-18). This is a documentation fix; no code change.

findings.claim: Duplicated `node_key` helper: redb_backend.rs's `node_storage_key` is a byte-for-byte re-implementation of store.rs's `node_key`. DRY violation invites a schema drift.
findings.detail: Both functions produce `b"n:" ++ cid.as_bytes()`. If someone ever changes the prefix (to add a version byte, say), and only updates one site, the inherent `RedbBackend::put_node` would write to the new schema while `NodeStore::get_node` on the same backend would read from the old schema, or vice versa. The comment in redb_backend.rs:688-691 acknowledges the duplication ('Duplicated rather than exported from store.rs to keep the key schema module-private') but the justification is thin — `pub(crate) fn node_key` in store.rs would preserve the same privacy envelope within the crate while guaranteeing both call sites use the same function.
findings.suggested_fix: Make `node_key` in store.rs `pub(crate)` and import it in redb_backend.rs; delete the duplicate `node_storage_key` + `NODE_PREFIX` const (lines 693-700). Two-line change. Also consider promoting all four key-schema functions (node_key, edge_key, edge_src_index_key, edge_tgt_index_key) into a `mod key_schema` inside `store.rs` or a fresh `crate::key_schema` module if more schemas arrive.

findings.claim: Edge body and edge indexes (`es:`/`et:`) are NOT written atomically in the blanket EdgeStore impl. A crash between the body put and the index puts leaves an inconsistent store.
findings.detail:
`put_edge` (lines 236-248) writes body via `self.put(&edge_key(&cid), &bytes)?` then issues two separate `self.put` calls for the source and target index keys. Each is a separate redb transaction. If the process dies between them, the edge body exists but no index entry — or partial index (src but not tgt). `edges_from` would then be silently inconsistent with `edges_to`.

`delete_edge` (lines 260-267) has the inverse problem: it deletes indexes then body in separate transactions.

The existing comment (lines 240-243) says 'the body/index pair is idempotent (re-putting the same edge writes identical bytes to the same keys), so ordering under a non-transactional call is not load-bearing at Phase 1'. This is true for `put_edge` (modulo crash recovery running through to completion), but NOT for `delete_edge` (once the body is gone there's nothing to re-resolve the index targets from). Also: `edges_from` will over-report during a crashed-partway `put_edge`.

The inherent `put_node`/`delete_node` got the atomic-write-transaction treatment (G2-B). Edges did not. The plan's R1 triage `P1.graph.indexes-on-write` tag covered both; G2-B commit message only claims 'indexes are populated through every inherent RedbBackend::put_node / delete_node in the same write transaction' — edges omitted.
findings.suggested_fix:
Two paths:
1. Fast: add inherent `put_edge` / `delete_edge` overrides on `RedbBackend` mirroring the node treatment — open one write transaction, insert body + two index entries, commit. Delete the inherent delegates at redb_backend.rs:431-457 and replace with real override bodies.
2. Slower but more principled: add a `begin_write` hook to the KVBackend trait so the blanket EdgeStore impl can open a transaction, route its body+index puts through it, and commit. This generalizes for future heterogeneous write batches.
G3 brings the full `transaction()` primitive; picking path (1) as a Phase-1 patch and relying on G3 for the generic path is the cleanest sequencing. Flag in triage.

findings.claim: ChangeEvent carries a single `label: String` (primary label) while the label index emits one entry per label on multi-label nodes — subscribers driven by the change stream may miss IVM-visible paths for non-primary labels.
findings.detail:
The `ChangeEvent` field docstring (store.rs:335-340) is upfront: 'Phase 1 uses `label: String` (single label) ... multi-label nodes still emit one ChangeEvent per event with the primary label filled in'. But the label-index maintainer (redb_backend.rs:332-334) inserts one multimap entry per label — so `get_by_label` hits multi-label nodes under each of their labels, while the change stream only surfaces the primary label. Downstream consumers like IVM views that build a label→CID index incrementally from the change stream would diverge from the on-disk label index for multi-label nodes.

Neither the G1 Node schema (`labels: Vec<String>`) nor the R1 invariants fix 'primary' in any machine-checkable way, so the 'primary label' is effectively `labels[0]`. This is a known asymmetry that is acceptable for Phase 1 scope (none of the 5 hand-written IVM views use multi-label nodes per the handler validation) but the docstring should explicitly call out the divergence with the label index as a known gap, not present it as a settled design.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the `ChangeEvent.label` docstring (store.rs:337-340) to add: 'Asymmetry note: the on-disk label index (see LABEL_INDEX_TABLE) emits one entry per label on multi-label nodes, while this event field carries only the primary label (labels[0]). IVM views that rebuild from the change stream can observe only the primary label path; views that also want non-primary labels must rebuild from the label index directly. R5 reconsiders for Phase 2 if the view surface demands it.'

findings.claim: Five `todo!()` stubs on the public API surface of RedbBackend. Documented as Phase 1 G3/G6/SC1 stubs, but `subscribe()` returns `ChangeReceiver` without a Result — a call from user code panics rather than returning an Err.
findings.detail:
The five stubs are: `put_node_with_context`, `transaction`, `transaction_with_deny_on_commit`, `subscribe`, `snapshot`. All five panic on invocation. Four return `Result<_, GraphError>` so a better Phase-1 shape would be to return `Err(GraphError::NotImplemented { landing_in_group: "G3-A" })` — still surfaces 'not implemented' but is catchable and doesn't unwind. `subscribe()` returns `ChangeReceiver` directly (no Result), so the same treatment isn't available without an API change.

The stubs being panicky is deliberate under the TDD red-phase contract (tests that hit them must fail red). But now that G2-B is in place and the red tests are correctly owned by G3, a real API user calling `subscribe()` in Phase 1 gets a runtime panic rather than a compile error or typed Err.
findings.suggested_fix:
Two options, both acceptable:
1. Leave as-is. The panic message is clear ('RedbBackend::subscribe — G3-A (Phase 1)') and Phase-1 users following the plan won't reach these before G3 lands. Accept the TDD red-phase discipline.
2. Swap `todo!()` for `Err(GraphError::NotImplemented { .. })` on the four Result-returning stubs and change `subscribe()` to return `Result<ChangeReceiver, GraphError>` for Phase 1. Adds one error variant + touches tests; probably not worth it unless Ben signals a preference. Recommend leaving as-is per the 'tests ARE the specification' rule; flag for awareness.

findings.claim: Six `From<redb::*> for GraphError` impls each stringify via `.to_string()` — the R1 triage `P1.graph.error-polymorphism` fix preserved the source chain at the KVBackend::Error boundary, but the concrete GraphError conversions still drop `std::error::Error::source`.
findings.detail:
G2-A closed 'error-polymorphism' by adding the associated `KVBackend::Error` type, which lets non-redb backends surface their errors without stringifying through `GraphError::Redb(String)`. Good.

But for redb itself, the existing six `From<redb::*> for GraphError { fn from(e) -> Self { GraphError::Redb(e.to_string()) } }` impls (lib.rs:121-150) still lose the source chain. `GraphError::Redb(String)` has no `#[source]` attribute because the payload is a String. The G2-A comment on GraphError explicitly said 'G2-B refines this to preserve the chain via #[from] + #[source]' — but G2-B did not land that refinement. The commit message for G2-B lists index tables and open-existing but not this.

Impact: an error chain like `redb::Error(redb::StorageError::Io(io::Error))` collapses to a string at the first From hop, and any `source()` caller upstream (a log middleware, a tracing event, an error-to-telemetry converter) sees an empty chain.
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a `GraphError::Redb { #[from] source: redb::Error }` variant (or one variant per redb error type if preferred), derive `#[source]`, and delete the stringification in the From impls. Likely requires adding a blanket `From<redb::DatabaseError|TransactionError|TableError|...>` → `redb::Error` using redb's own error hierarchy, or keeping one From per redb error kind if redb's types don't unify. Ten-line change; cleans up the R1 debt G2-A flagged and G2-B deferred.

findings.claim: lib.rs docstring still describes `indexes` module as 'G5' / separately owned, but `indexes` is now a `pub mod` and `RedbBackend` IS re-exported at crate root. Mild drift between comment and code.
findings.detail:
Line 19-20 of the lib.rs docstring reads: 'this module — the concrete [`RedbBackend`], [`GraphError`], and the Phase-1 stubs (`Transaction`, `WriteContext`, `SnapshotHandle`, `ChangeReceiver`) owned by G2-B / G3 / G5 / G6.'

But `RedbBackend` now lives in `redb_backend.rs`, not in this module — it's re-exported at line 34. The docstring claim that 'this module' owns `RedbBackend` is stale. Similarly, `indexes` is declared on line 29 (`pub mod indexes`) but not re-exported at crate root (lines 33-35), leaving `get_by_label` / `get_by_property` on `RedbBackend` but the `indexes` module itself accessible only as `benten_graph::indexes::*` — which only exposes the crate-private constants externally for no external consumer.

Neither is wrong-wrong. Both are doc drift from the G2-B refactor.
findings.suggested_fix:
1. Update lib.rs:18-20 to read 'this module — [`GraphError`] and the Phase-1 stubs (`Transaction`, `WriteContext`, `SnapshotHandle`, `ChangeReceiver`) owned by G3 / G6. The concrete [`RedbBackend`] lives in [`redb_backend`]; label and property-value indexes in [`indexes`].'
2. Consider making `pub mod indexes` either `pub(crate)` (since all items are crate-private) or wiring at least the public-facing constants through. Recommend `pub(crate)` since the table-definition constants are an implementation detail.

findings.claim: `delete_node` reads the existing Node OUTSIDE the write transaction, then opens a write transaction to remove body+indexes. A concurrent `put_node` of the same CID between the read and the transaction could cause the index cleanup to target stale label/property keys.
findings.detail:
Line 397: `let existing = <Self as NodeStore>::get_node(self, cid)?` — this opens a read transaction, snapshots, closes. Line 400: separate write transaction begins.

With content-addressed hashing, a CID uniquely identifies the (labels, properties) payload, so any re-put of the same CID by definition re-writes the same bytes to the same index keys. The attack surface is thinner than it first appears: a concurrent `put_node(same_node) + delete_node(same_node)` race doesn't produce index corruption because the index keys are identical on both paths. OK.

The surviving concern is subtler: if `delete_node` observes `existing = Some(node_v1)` but another writer has meanwhile `put_node(node_v1)` again, the delete's `remove(label_idx, label_bytes, cid.as_bytes())` will succeed — redb multimap remove is idempotent — but the concurrent put also re-inserted, so now the index is effectively recreated with the stale-view delete interleaved. End state depends on redb's per-table serialization order, which is single-writer inside the transaction. Since both operations target the same keys with same values, the final state converges. Whew — this is actually safe under the content-addressed invariant.

Flagging for visibility: the safety argument RELIES on content-addressed CID uniqueness, which isn't documented at this call site. If Phase 2 ever introduces mutable node identities (anchors with CURRENT pointer), this read-then-write pattern becomes a race.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a safety comment above line 397 in redb_backend.rs: '// Read-outside-txn is safe here because the CID is content-addressed — any concurrent `put_node(same CID)` writes identical bytes to identical index keys, so our read-view index-key set cannot diverge from the current state. This invariant will break for future mutable identities (e.g., Anchor.CURRENT pointer in Phase 2); re-evaluate then.' No code change needed now.

findings.claim: Inherent methods `get_node`/`put_edge`/`get_edge`/`edges_from`/`edges_to` are one-line trait delegates. Audit concern: the doctest-level API may hide the existence of the trait blanket impls entirely.
findings.detail:
Audit concern G2-A #2 confirmed. Each inherent method at lines 367-369, 431-457 is `<Self as TraitName>::method(self, …)` — pure trait delegation. The rationale (per audit prompt) is that tests import `RedbBackend` without importing `NodeStore` / `EdgeStore`, so the inherent method keeps the test site compiling.

The hazard is that the public-facing doctest examples (e.g., the `get_node` doctest at redb_backend.rs:356-366) show callers using `b.get_node(&cid)` with no `use benten_graph::NodeStore;` import. This is fine when `RedbBackend` is the only backend in the world. It becomes a documentation trap the moment a second backend (in-memory mock, WASM peer-fetch) lands — those backends have no inherent methods, so examples become `use benten_graph::NodeStore; let x = mem.get_node(&cid);` — and users who learned the API from `RedbBackend` doctests will hit 'no method named get_node' compile errors when they try the pattern with any other backend.

Is that a blocker for G2? No. It's a signal that the doctests (and eventually the user-facing READMEs) should show at least one example via the trait to teach the pattern correctly.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one doctest example on the NodeStore trait (store.rs:108-135) that demonstrates the generic pattern: `fn stash<T: NodeStore<Error=GraphError>>(store: &T, n: &Node) -> Result<Cid, GraphError> { store.put_node(n) }`. Keeps the inherent methods for ergonomics but teaches the trait surface. No code change required on RedbBackend.

audit_of_implementer_known_compromises.g2_a_1_scanresult_not_boxed: Concern upheld — see g2-cr-6. Docstring overpromises migration path. Flagged as minor; no code change required beyond doc honesty update.
audit_of_implementer_known_compromises.g2_a_2_inherent_methods_delegates: Concern partly upheld — see g2-cr-14. Minor documentation hazard, not a defect. Add a trait-level doctest example.
audit_of_implementer_known_compromises.g2_a_3_coreerror_serialize_decode: Acceptable. CoreError::Serialize is a reasonable catch-all for bytes<->type failures; parallel enum variant would be nicer but not load-bearing.
audit_of_implementer_known_compromises.g2_a_4_clippy_useless_conversion_suppres: Acceptable. Suppression is well-justified with an explicit reason comment and scoped to the blanket impls only.
audit_of_implementer_known_compromises.g2_b_1_durability_group_collapse: Concern upheld — see g2-cr-3. Major finding; user-visible enum variant with misleading performance docstring.
audit_of_implementer_known_compromises.g2_b_2_open_existing_toctou: Concern upheld but accepted — see g2-cr-4. Minor. Phase 1 acceptable; Phase 3 re-evaluation required.
audit_of_implementer_known_compromises.g2_b_3_inherent_put_node_delete_node_ind: Concern upheld and ELEVATED to major — see g2-cr-1. Latent footgun where trait dispatch silently misses index maintenance. Recommend either inherent-only NodeStore or IndexedNodeStore supertrait.
audit_of_implementer_known_compromises.g2_b_4_test_authoring_bug_flagged: Concern upheld — see g2-cr-5. Major: test should be patched (2 lines) with clear triage-log entry; fix is a typo, not a spec change.

api_stability_for_g3.transaction_stub_shape: OK — current `Transaction<'a>` stub uses PhantomData and exposes put_node/put_edge/transaction(nested). G3 will add real body; signatures compatible with closure-based API (F: FnOnce(&mut Transaction<'_>) -> Result<R, GraphError>).
api_stability_for_g3.change_receiver: OK — ChangeReceiver::recv() -> Result<ChangeEvent, GraphError> shape is stable; G3 replaces the unit struct with channel state.
api_stability_for_g3.write_context: OK for G4 integration — WriteContext::new(label) + privileged_for_engine_api() + enforce_system_zone stub matches CapabilityPolicy::check_write(&WriteContext) signature expectation. is_privileged bool + label: String is additively extensible in G3; no incompatibility.
api_stability_for_g3.snapshot_handle: OK — unit struct with Drop + stub methods; G6 adds fields without breaking callers.
api_stability_for_g3.net_assessment: G3 can wire Transaction + ChangeReceiver + WriteContext additively. No breaking changes required to G2 types. RedbBackend::subscribe returning ChangeReceiver directly (not Result) may want revisiting when channel init can fail in G3 — flag as minor-in-G3, not a G2 problem.

module_boundary_assessment.backend_rs: Good — trait surface + supporting types. No circular imports. Module docstring accurate.
module_boundary_assessment.store_rs: Good — blanket impls over KVBackend, plus ChangeSubscriber/ChangeEvent. Key schema helpers (node_key, edge_key, edge_src_index_key, edge_tgt_index_key) are crate-private (private fn). See g2-cr-7 for DRY with redb_backend.rs.
module_boundary_assessment.redb_backend_rs: Does more than its name implies — it also hosts the index maintainers (put_node/delete_node inherent overrides), which are arguably a separate concern from the KVBackend impl. Not a blocker; indexes are redb-specific because of MultimapTable, so coupling is justified. Comment block at lines 290-298 makes the asymmetry clear.
module_boundary_assessment.indexes_rs: Good — thin, focused on key encoding + table definitions. Module itself should probably be `pub(crate)` rather than `pub mod` since no items are externally accessible (see g2-cr-12).
module_boundary_assessment.lib_rs: Now appropriately thin (G2-B extracted 345 lines). Still holds error types and G3/G6 stubs which is appropriate — they don't fit in backend/store/redb_backend/indexes. No circular imports detected.
module_boundary_assessment.verdict: Module split is sensible. One DRY violation (g2-cr-7), one privacy nit (g2-cr-12), zero structural issues.

doc_completeness_spot_check.pub_items_without_docs: None found. Every pub struct/enum/trait/fn/method has a `///` docstring.
doc_completeness_spot_check.crate_level_lib_rs_doc: Partially stale after G2-B extraction — see g2-cr-12.
doc_completeness_spot_check.examples_on_public_methods: Present on all RedbBackend::open* constructors and on put_node/get_node/delete_node/get_by_label/get_by_property. Doctest example on the NodeStore trait itself is missing — see g2-cr-14.

error_handling_spot_check.unwrap_or_expect_in_production: None found in src/; only in `#[cfg(test)]` blocks and doctests which is per-policy.
error_handling_spot_check.graph_error_code_mapping: Complete — every GraphError variant has an arm in GraphError::code(). ErrorCode::Unknown("graph_internal") is a soft fallback for Redb/Decode which loses specificity; see g2-cr-11.
error_handling_spot_check.error_message_stability: Display impls are stable strings with `{0}` / `{field}` formatting — OK for consumer matching.

index_correctness_spot_check.key_distinct_from_node_key: OK — label_index uses multimap table `benten_label_index` (separate table from NODES_TABLE); prop_index uses `benten_prop_index`. Distinct tables, no collision risk with `n:CID` keys.
index_correctness_spot_check.delete_atomicity: OK — delete_node opens one write transaction, removes body + label index entries + property index entries, commits. All atomic per redb serializable isolation.
index_correctness_spot_check.value_serialization_deterministic: OK — value_index_bytes uses serde_ipld_dagcbor::to_vec (DAG-CBOR canonical by construction).
index_correctness_spot_check.multimap_vs_normal_table: Appropriate — each label/property-key has multiple CID values; MultimapTable is the right shape.
index_correctness_spot_check.length_prefix_on_property_key: OK — property_index_key uses u32_be length prefix on label + prop_name segments (indexes.rs:62-73), preempts `(label="Po", prop="stviews")` vs `(label="Post", prop="views")` collision.
index_correctness_spot_check.concurrent_read_between_put_and_index: N/A — both node body and indexes commit in the same redb write transaction; redb serializable isolation guarantees no reader sees partial state.

concurrent_safety_spot_check.send_sync: RedbBackend is Send+Sync (redb::Database is Send+Sync, Durability is Copy). KVBackend trait bound is `Send + Sync` (backend.rs:245). OK.
concurrent_safety_spot_check.put_node_atomicity: OK — single write transaction for body + indexes.
concurrent_safety_spot_check.arc_shareability: OK — doc example at redb_backend.rs:103-105 shows `Arc<RedbBackend>` pattern; redb's &self API on Database supports multi-reader + single-writer.
concurrent_safety_spot_check.potential_footgun: See g2-cr-13 — delete_node reads outside txn then writes inside. Safe under content-addressed invariant; flag for Phase 2 re-eval.

doctests_spot_check.count: 11/11 pass per implementer report, verified via `cargo test --doc -p benten-graph`.
doctests_spot_check.coverage: open_existing, open_existing_with_durability, open_or_create, open_or_create_with_durability, open, put_node, get_node, delete_node, get_by_label, get_by_property, MemBackend on KVBackend trait.
doctests_spot_check.typical_usage_demonstrated: Yes — put_node + get_by_label + get_by_property doctests chain `open_or_create -> put_node -> query`, demonstrating the intended pattern.
doctests_spot_check.gap: No doctest exercises the trait surface (NodeStore/EdgeStore generic) — see g2-cr-14.

cargo_toml_assessment.new_deps_added: None in G2 commits — redb, serde_ipld_dagcbor, thiserror, benten-core were already present.
cargo_toml_assessment.dev_deps: tempfile, proptest, criterion, benten-engine (for cross-process integration test). Dev-dep cycle on benten-engine is safe + explicitly commented.
cargo_toml_assessment.bin_target: write-canonical-and-exit bin properly gated with test=false, bench=false. Cycle-safe.
cargo_toml_assessment.bench_targets: 4 benches declared with harness=false for criterion. OK.

wasm32_target.status: verified clean via `cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown -p benten-graph`.
wasm32_target.cfg_gating: None required — redb compiles on wasm32 per the workspace configuration. No `#[cfg(target_family = \"wasm\")]` gates visible in G2 code. KVBackend trait surface is the intended swap-point for WASM peer-fetch backend (Phase 2+), per the lib.rs docstring.

test_file_edits.grep_result: No test file edits in commits 0abe47c or 58d034b — verified via `git show --stat 0abe47c 58d034b | grep tests/`.
test_file_edits.per_tdd_policy: G2 implementers respected the red-phase contract — tests untouched, all greens came from production code landing.
test_file_edits.flagged_test_bug: See g2-cr-5 — one test needs a 2-line fix in a follow-up; not landed in G2.

stale_comments_todos.todo_calls: 5 in non-stub paths? No — all `todo!()` calls are in documented Phase-1 stubs (G3/G6/SC1 scope): put_node_with_context, transaction, transaction_with_deny_on_commit, subscribe, snapshot, and the 3 stubs on Transaction/SnapshotHandle/ChangeReceiver/WriteContext. All labeled and owned by their target group. No stray todo!() in implemented code.
stale_comments_todos.box_dyn_iterator_references: Grep clean — no stale references to Box<dyn Iterator> outside of the explicit `scan-iterator` R1-triage tag comments, which correctly reference ScanResult now.

notes:
G2 is a solid multi-commit refactor. G2-A correctly reshaped the trait surface per R1 triage P1.graph.{error-polymorphism,scan-iterator,node-store-trait,doctests}. G2-B correctly extracted RedbBackend + added open-existing/open-or-create split + DurabilityMode wiring + atomic index maintenance. Pipeline green (fmt/clippy/check/check-wasm32/doctest). 55/69 nextest green with 14 expected reds for G3/G6/SC1 scope plus 1 flagged test-authoring bug.

RECOMMENDED DISPOSITION: `revise`. Three major findings require action before G3 dispatch:
  - g2-cr-1 (NodeStore trait-dispatch index-miss footgun) — pick fix path (a) or (b) in findings.
  - g2-cr-3 (DurabilityMode::Group silent collapse) — add `# Phase 1 note` to docstring, optionally emit a tracing warn.
  - g2-cr-5 (test-authoring bug in open_or_create_idempotent_on_existing_file) — 2-line fix + triage log entry.

Eight minor findings are documentation/DRY/hygiene polish that can be batched into a G2 fix-pass or deferred to R4b depending on Ben's preference.

API stability for G3 is clean: Transaction / ChangeReceiver / WriteContext / SnapshotHandle stubs are additively extensible without breaking G2 signatures. G3 can proceed on current G2 foundation.

OUT OF SCOPE: benten-caps WIP clippy failure in `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets` (parallel G4 fix-pass). Verified benten-graph itself is clippy-clean via `cargo clippy -p benten-graph --all-targets -- -D warnings`.
